articles.title: Redistricting, proof-of-citizenship fights and court rulings are redefining the 2026 election map
articles.summary: A new round of state-level voting rules, lawsuits and map fights is testing how much control legislatures and governors can exert over the 2026 midterms before ballots are cast.

articles.title: As governments pump money back into culture, the fight is over who gets to belong
articles.summary: From London’s rescue package to U.S. museum anxiety and Broadway’s pricey rebound, arts leaders are confronting a bigger question than survival: whether public culture is still broad-based—or becoming a luxury good.

articles.title: How AI Data Centers Became an Electric-Grid Problem
articles.summary: The boom in artificial intelligence is pushing utilities, regulators, and chipmakers into a faster, messier race for power than the grid was built to handle.

articles.title: Gas Shock Rewrites the Cost-of-Living Story, Forcing a New Household Arithmetic
articles.summary: March inflation jumped sharply as gasoline prices surged in the wake of the Iran war, complicating the Fed’s path and squeezing budgets just as rents, mortgage costs and tariffs were still working through the economy.

articles.title: As federal aid rules shift again, colleges and families brace for another year of education-policy whiplash
articles.summary: New FAFSA systems, looming federal loan changes and the end of pandemic aid are colliding with enrollment declines, chronic absenteeism and staffing strain across schools and colleges.

articles.title: Measles is back in the U.S. at the start of spring, and the policy response is under strain
articles.summary: CDC said the United States had 1,714 confirmed measles cases by April 9, 2026, while flu, RSV and COVID surveillance show why vaccination, early detection and public trust matter more than ever.

articles.title: The moral bargain of age gates: Why child safety is becoming a surveillance state in miniature
articles.summary: Across the U.S. and Europe, politicians and platforms are racing to verify users’ ages online. The promise is protection; the cost may be a lasting expansion of identity checks, data collection, and algorithmic judgment for everyone.

articles.title: Alaska’s thawing permafrost is moving faster than the models some planners still use
articles.summary: New research suggests parts of Alaska’s frozen ground are releasing water and carbon faster than expected, with implications for coasts, roads, habitat and climate feedbacks — and for how much confidence scientists should place in older baseline assumptions.

articles.title: Lebanon’s war spillover is turning a border into a regional stress test
articles.summary: A fragile de-escalation on Lebanon’s front has shifted the crisis from bombardment to movement: Syrians and Lebanese crossing back and forth, aid pipelines straining, and neighbors bracing for a longer humanitarian shock.

articles.title: Trump’s tariff refunds are not a technical fix. They are a confession.
articles.summary: The Supreme Court’s tariff ruling should end the fantasy that America can be made cheaper, stronger and more sovereign by executive whim. Congress should reclaim trade power—and stop treating import taxes as a shortcut around actual industrial policy.

articles.title: As AI data centers spread, the real fight is over power, water and who pays
articles.summary: A nationwide backlash is forcing AI’s infrastructure boom into city councils, statehouses and utility hearings, where the costs and benefits are being disputed in public.

articles.title: Hormuz Standoff Forces a Brutal Stress Test of Deterrence, Alliances and the World Economy
articles.summary: As the Strait of Hormuz turns from leverage point to active battleground, governments are discovering how much of today’s geopolitics runs through one narrow waterway—and how little room they have to absorb the shock.

articles.title: Hollywood’s movie theaters get a rare boost — and a warning — at CinemaCon 2026
articles.summary: A stronger-than-expected spring box office and Paramount Skydance’s vows to keep films in theaters longer are reviving old arguments over how Hollywood should sell movies. But the proposed Paramount-Warner Bros. tie-up is turning that optimism into a test of whether more scale means more screens — or fewer choices.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial illustration for: Hollywood’s movie theaters get a rare boost — and a warning — at CinemaCon 2026. AI-generated illustration by The Press.

articles.title: TikTok’s New Power Shift Is Rewriting Pop Culture Economics
articles.summary: As TikTok secures a U.S. future, AI music, creator monetization fights and platform bundling reshape how hits are made, sold and measured.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial illustration for: TikTok’s New Power Shift Is Rewriting Pop Culture Economics. AI-generated illustration by The Press.

articles.title: The New Status Economy of Indie Sleaze
articles.summary: From thrift racks and resale carts to vinyl bins and flash photography, a once-niche 2000s aesthetic has become a live marketplace, a youth language, and a fight over who gets to define authenticity.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial illustration for: The New Status Economy of Indie Sleaze. AI-generated illustration by The Press.
